planting.note: Plant bulbs 6-8 inches deep in fall in very well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; giant allium does not tolerate wet, waterlogged soils and the bulbs must be kept dry during summer dormancy — it is best where soils dry out after flowering. Plant in drifts of 5-9 bulbs for best ornamental impact.

directions: Plant bulbs 6-8 inches deep in fall in very well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; giant allium does not tolerate wet, waterlogged soils and the bulbs must be kept dry during summer dormancy — it is best where soils dry out after flowering. Allium giganteum is the largest of the ornamental alliums, producing massive, round, 5-6 inch diameter spherical flower heads of hundreds of small, rosy-purple to violet-purple star-shaped flowers on tall (3-5 foot), rigid stems in late May and June — one of the most architectural and spectacular effects in the early summer bulb garden. The dried seed heads retain their spherical shape beautifully for months after bloom and are outstanding in dried arrangements; they also provide seeds for finches and sparrows in fall and winter. Plant in drifts of 5-9 bulbs for best ornamental impact.

planting.note: Plant container-grown stock in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to poor soil in partial shade; blue flax lily tolerates full sun in cooler climates but requires afternoon shade in zone 9-11 heat.

directions: Plant container-grown stock in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to poor soil in partial shade; blue flax lily tolerates full sun in cooler climates but requires afternoon shade in zone 9-11 heat. Dianella caerulea is an outstanding, low-maintenance evergreen perennial from eastern Australia with arching, strap-like, dark-green leaves and airy panicles of small, blue-purple, six-petaled flowers on slender stems in spring and summer, followed by distinctive, bright, electric-blue to indigo berries in fall and winter that are one of the most striking ornamental fruit colors in the plant world; the berries persist attractively for months. It forms handsome, slowly expanding clumps and is an outstanding drought-tolerant, shade-tolerant groundcover for warm-climate gardens in zones 8-11, where it is used extensively as a border edging and under-tree groundcover. Very deer-resistant.

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container stock in spring or fall in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; European hazel is wind-pollinated and requires at least two genetically different plants (or two named cultivars) for reliable nut production — one plant alone may produce few or no nuts.

directions: Plant bare-root or container stock in spring or fall in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; European hazel is wind-pollinated and requires at least two genetically different plants (or two named cultivars) for reliable nut production — one plant alone may produce few or no nuts. Corylus avellana is the hazel of European woodlands and hedgerows, grown for millennia for its nutritious hazelnuts (cobnuts and filberts in commerce); flexible, coppiced stems have been used for furniture, wattling, and charcoal production since prehistoric times; and the soft, round catkins that dangle from every bare stem in February and March are one of the earliest and most important pollen sources of spring for emerging native bees, including queen bumblebees and early Andrena mining bees. The large, rounded leaves turn golden-yellow in fall; the twisted cultivar 'Contorta' (Harry Lauder's Walking Stick) is a popular ornamental selection. Plant two different cultivars for best nut yield.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in early spring (just as growth begins) or fall, setting just below the soil surface in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to moderately wet soil in partial shade to full sun; Siberian iris tolerates wet soils and partial shade far better than bearded iris and requires much less maintenance.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in early spring (just as growth begins) or fall, setting just below the soil surface in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to moderately wet soil in partial shade to full sun; Siberian iris tolerates wet soils and partial shade far better than bearded iris and requires much less maintenance. Iris sibirica is widely considered the most refined and garden-worthy of all the iris species, producing slender, grass-like, attractive foliage throughout the season and elegant, five-inch flowers with gracefully drooping falls in shades of violet-blue, purple, white, pink, and yellow in late May to June; unlike bearded iris, it rarely needs dividing (every 5-7 years only), is disease-resistant, and is virtually deer-proof. It thrives at pond margins, in rain gardens, and in consistently moist borders; the attractive, upright seedpod clusters provide winter interest and self-seeding; it is an outstanding cut flower with slender, upright foliage that remains attractive all season.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in early spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial shade; grass-leaved iris is one of the very few irises that performs well in partial shade and is native to shaded woodland margins in southern Europe.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in early spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial shade; grass-leaved iris is one of the very few irises that performs well in partial shade and is native to shaded woodland margins in southern Europe. Iris graminea is a delightful, small-flowered iris of European woodland edges and shaded grasslands, forming compact clumps of narrow, grass-like leaves among which the small, purple-and-white, typical iris-form flowers are half-hidden in late spring — but the flowers make themselves unmistakably known by their extraordinary fruity fragrance, described universally as exactly like ripe Victoria plums or plum tarts, one of the most surprisingly sweet and specific scents of any iris. The plant is compact, slow-spreading, and absolutely trouble-free in partial shade; use it at the front of a shaded border or beneath deciduous shrubs where its fragrance can be appreciated when passing. It will not tolerate drought or alkaline soil.
